title: Ronald Bronstein and Josh Safdie on Editing Two Crucial Sections of Marty Supreme
text: Editors and co-writers Ronald Bronstein and Josh Safdie earned Oscar nominations for their work on Marty Supreme, directed by Safdie and starring Timothée Chalamet. The film follows a table tennis champion navigating professional decline and personal reinvention. Bronstein highlighted how a crucial scene required repositioning rather than trimming. Initially, a Paris confrontation between protagonist Marty and Milton Rockwell interrupted the Globetrotters montage, which depicted Marty's downfall through touring cities. In the cut, it was fatal as the dialogue scene cleaved the montage in two and diminished emotional impact. Rather than cutting the scene, they moved it. The solution was radical but obvious in hindsight: move the Paris confrontation to the head of the sequence, before the music cue and montage begin. This reordering transformed the exchange from explanatory to tense, allowing the audience to experience confusion before revelation. Safdie described the Japan sequence as intentionally constructed to build tension methodically, referencing Eisenstein's approach, designing the edit to reach an apex of bottled energy only to give way to a great release upon victory. A single Steadicam shot deliberately altered pacing, holding shots longer to create reflective space.
